perturbation.original_text: WHEREAS , the Parties may find it necessary to provide each other certain Confidential Information (as hereinafter defined) in connection with: (i) negotiating the Supplier Agreement or otherwise exploring the possibility of a business relationship between the Parties; (ii) furthering the intent or purposes of any currently existing or future business relationship between the Parties; and (iii) furthering the intent or purposes of the Supplier Agreement and any other existing or future written agreement between the Parties (collectively, the “Business Purpose ”).
perturbation.changed_text: WHEREAS , the Parties may find it necessary to provide each other information in connection with: (i) negotiating the Supplier Agreement or otherwise exploring the possibility of a business relationship between the Parties; (ii) furthering the intent or purposes of any currently existing or future business relationship between the Parties; and (iii) furthering the intent or purposes of the Supplier Agreement and any other existing or future written agreement between the Parties (collectively, the “Business Purpose ”).
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'certain Confidential Information (as hereinafter defined)' the text now references simply 'information'. However, the rest of the document relies on the definition of 'Confidential Information' and the obligations associated with it, found in Section 7. This omission creates a contradiction because the Parties' obligations concerning information exchange become vague. This change contradicts Section 2, where it states Each Party hereby agrees that any Confidential Information received by it will be used solely for the Business Purpose, and that such information will be kept confidential by such Party
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each Party hereby agrees that any Confidential Information received by it will be used solely for the Business Purpose, and that such information will be kept confidential by such Party; provided, however, that such Confidential Information may be disclosed to the Representatives of the Receiving Party on a need to know basis to the extent necessary to further the Business Purpose (it being understood that such Representatives shall be informed by the Receiving Party of the confidential nature of such information and shall be directed to treat such information confidentially and that the Rec eiving Party shall take all reasonable measures to restrain
perturbation.justification: The original text refers to 'certain Confidential Information (as hereinafter defined)', which aligns with the contradicted text that discusses 'Confidential Information'. The changed text omits 'certain Confidential Information (as hereinafter defined)' and instead refers to 'information'. This omission creates a contradiction because the contradicted text in Section 2 specifically refers to 'Confidential Information' and imposes obligations based on that defined term. By removing the reference to 'Confidential Information' in the changed text, the scope of information covered becomes vague, potentially conflicting with the specific confidentiality obligations outlined in Section 2.

perturbation.original_text: 3. Export Control Status. Each Party specifically acknowledges that use or disclosure of the Confidential Information may be subject to U.S. Government’s export laws and regulatio ns, included but not limited to, the International Traffic in Arms Regulations (ITAR) and the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) and warrants that such Party shall make no use or disclosure of the Confidential Information contrary to such laws or othe r applicable laws. Any Confidential Information disclosed, by either P arty, will be marked with its control status under the regulations. In the case of information disclosed verbally the control status will be given prior to discussions and the subseque nt memo will be marked wi th that status. The Receiving Party certifies that no portion of the Confidential Information will be exported or transferred, whether directly or indirectly, without first complying strictly and fully with all export controls that may be imposed on such information by the United States government or any country or organization or nations within whose jurisdiction the Receiving Party operates or does business. The Receiving Party shall indemnify and hold the Disclosing Party harmles s against all claims, demands, damages, costs, fines, penalties, attorneys’ fees, and all other expenses arising from failure of the Receiving Party to comply with this Section 3 or applicable export control laws and regulations.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Export Control Status. Each Party specifically acknowledges that use or disclosure of information may be subject to U.S. Government’s export laws and regulatio ns, included but not limited to, the International Traffic in Arms Regulations (ITAR) and the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) and warrants that such Party shall make no use or disclosure of information contrary to such laws or othe r applicable laws. Any information disclosed, by either P arty, will be marked with its control status under the regulations. In the case of information disclosed verbally the control status will be given prior to discussions and the subseque nt memo will be marked wi th that status. The Receiving Party certifies that no portion of the information will be exported or transferred, whether directly or indirectly, without first complying strictly and fully with all export controls that may be imposed on such information by the United States government or any country or organization or nations within whose jurisdiction the Receiving Party operates or does business. The Receiving Party shall indemnify and hold the Disclosing Party harmles s against all claims, demands, damages, costs, fines, penalties, attorneys’ fees, and all other expenses arising from failure of the Receiving Party to comply with this Section 3 or applicable export control laws and regulations.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'Confidential' throughout Section 3, the protections and restrictions now apply to all 'information,' not just 'Confidential Information.' This broadens the scope in a way that contradicts the intended purpose of the agreement, which is to protect specific sensitive data. This contradicts section 7 where they defined Confidential Information, but the change in Section 3, makes the Export Control applies to all kind of information
perturbation.contradicted_text: For the purposes of this Agreement, the term “Confidential Information” shall mean any information furnished to the Receiving Party or its Representatives, whether oral, written or visual, and regardless of the manner in which it is furnished, by or on behalf of the Disclosing Party or its Representative, and shall include , without limitation: a) information constituting a "Trade S ecret", as defined in Idaho Code, Title 48, Chapter 8; Idaho Trade Secrets Act, as amended from time to time, and will include technical or non -technical data, a formal, a pattern, a compilation, a program, a software program, a device, a method, a technique, a drawing, a process, financial data, financial plans, product plans, non -public forecasts, studies, projections, analyses, all customer data of any kind, or a list of actual or potential customers or suppliers, business and contractual relationships, or any information similar to the foregoing which (i) derives economic value, ac tual or potential, from not being generally known and not readily ascertainable by proper means to other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (ii) is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to ma intain its secrecy. Trade Secrets will include any information meeting the foregoing criteria which belongs to third parties and which the Disclosing Party under this Agreement is obligated to hold in confidence; b) the non -public fina ncial statements of a p arty; c) any confidential and proprietary information which the Disclosing P arty obtains from another person or entity under an obligati on of confidence and which the Disclosing P arty treats as proprietary; and d) any valuable, secret b usiness information, other than Trade Secrets, including but not limited to all specifications, drawings, sketches, models, samples, reports, plans, forecasts, and all other technical, financial or business data that is designated or identified as confidential at the time of disclo sure or by its nature is clearly recognizable as confidential information to a reasonably prudent person with knowledge of the Disclosing Party’s business and industry All information or material that is disclosed by a Disclosing Party to a Receiving Party before, on or after the date hereof shall only be protected as Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party: (i) if in writing other tangible form, such information is marked as “confidential” or “proprietary; (ii) if oral or in other intangible form, such information or material is identified to the Receiving Party in writing as confidential or proprietary within sixty (60) days of the date of such disclosure; or (iii) if due to the character or nature of such information or material, a reasonable per son receiving such information or material under like circumstances would believe that Disclosing Party c onsidered such information or material proprietary or confidential.
perturbation.justification: The change removes 'Confidential' throughout Section 3, broadening the scope from 'Confidential Information' to all 'information.' This contradicts the definition of 'Confidential Information' in the contradicted text (Section 7), as the export controls now apply to all information, not just the defined 'Confidential Information,' creating an in-text contradiction and impacting the agreement's purpose.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Return . Within ten (10) calendar days of written request by the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party shall return to the Disclosing Party, or at the Disclosing Party's option, shall destroy, any physical and written records containing such Confidential Information, whether such physical and written records were sup plied or made available by the Disclosing Party or were prepared by the R ecipient or its Representatives from information sup plied or made available by the Disclosing Party. In such event, the R ecipient shall certify in writing to the Disclosing P arty that such physical or written materials have been returned o r destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Return . Within ten (10) calendar days of written request by the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party shall return to the Disclosing Party, or at the Disclosing Party's option, shall destroy, any physical and written records containing information, whether such physical and written records were sup plied or made available by the Disclosing Party or were prepared by the R ecipient or its Representatives from information sup plied or made available by the Disclosing Party. In such event, the R ecipient shall certify in writing to the Disclosing P arty that such physical or written materials have been returned o r destroyed.
perturbation.explanation: By replacing 'Confidential Information' with 'information' in Section 5, the obligation to return or destroy materials expands to all information, not just confidential information. This creates an in-text contradiction because the rest of the agreement is focused on protecting Confidential Information, creating the impression that this obligation will also only be for Confidential Information. This contradicts section 7 because the action of Returning/Destroying information would have to consider ALL information, and not only the one defined in Section 7.
perturbation.contradicted_text: For the purposes of this Agreement, the term “Confidential Information” shall mean any information furnished to the Receiving Party or its Representatives, whether oral, written or visual, and regardless of the manner in which it is furnished, by or on behalf of the Disclosing Party or its Representative, and shall include , without limitation: a) information constituting a "Trade S ecret", as defined in Idaho Code, Title 48, Chapter 8; Idaho Trade Secrets Act, as amended from time to time, and will include technical or non -technical data, a formal, a pattern, a compilation, a program, a software program, a device, a method, a technique, a drawing, a process, financial data, financial plans, product plans, non -public forecasts, studies, projections, analyses, all customer data of any kind, or a list of actual or potential customers or suppliers, business and contractual relationships, or any information similar to the foregoing which (i) derives economic value, ac tual or potential, from not being generally known and not readily ascertainable by proper means to other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (ii) is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to ma intain its secrecy. Trade Secrets will include any information meeting the foregoing criteria which belongs to third parties and which the Disclosing Party under this Agreement is obligated to hold in confidence; b) the non -public fina ncial statements of a p arty; c) any confidential and proprietary information which the Disclosing P arty obtains from another person or entity under an obligati on of confidence and which the Disclosing P arty treats as proprietary; and d) any valuable, secret b usiness information, other than Trade Secrets, including but not limited to all specifications, drawings, sketches, models, samples, reports, plans, forecasts, and all other technical, financial or business data that is designated or identified as confidential at the time of disclo sure or by its nature is clearly recognizable as confidential information to a reasonably prudent person with knowledge of the Disclosing Party’s business and industry All information or material that is disclosed by a Disclosing Party to a Receiving Party before, on or after the date hereof shall only be protected as Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party: (i) if in writing other tangible form, such information is marked as “confidential” or “proprietary; (ii) if oral or in other intangible form, such information or material is identified to the Receiving Party in writing as confidential or proprietary within sixty (60) days of the date of such disclosure; or (iii) if due to the character or nature of such information or material, a reasonable per son receiving such information or material under like circumstances would believe that Disclosing Party c onsidered such information or material proprietary or confidential.
perturbation.justification: The original text limits the return/destruction obligation to 'Confidential Information'. The changed text broadens this obligation to all 'information'. The contradicted text defines 'Confidential Information' specifically, creating a contradiction because the Receiving Party is now obligated to return or destroy all information, not just that defined as Confidential Information in the 'Contradicted Text' section, therefore the obligations are not consistent within the agreement. This creates a direct conflict, making the scope of the return/destroy obligation ambiguous and contradictory to the definition of 'Confidential Information'.

perturbation.original_text: 6. Continuing Obligation . The confidentiality obligations under this Agreement shall re main in effect for the longer of (i) five (5) years after the termination of this Agreement, or (ii) five (5) years after the expiration or termina tion of th e Business Purpose. Notwithstanding the foregoing, with respect to Confidential Information that constitutes a Trade Secret, the confidentiality obligations under this Agreement shall survive as long as the information disclosed continues to constitute a T rade Secret under applicable law.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. Continuing Obligation . The confidentiality obligations under this Agreement shall re main in effect for the longer of (i) five (5) years after the termination of this Agreement, or (ii) five (5) years after the expiration or termina tion of th e Business Purpose. Notwithstanding the foregoing, with respect to information that constitutes a Trade Secret, the confidentiality obligations under this Agreement shall survive as long as the information disclosed continues to constitute a T rade Secret under applicable law.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'Confidential' before 'Information' in Section 6 creates a contradiction regarding the survival of confidentiality obligations. The agreement initially intends specific protection for 'Confidential Information'. But by omitting 'Confidential,' the survival clause ambiguously extends to any information that might be considered a Trade Secret. This contradicts Section 7, which defines 'Confidential Information' and intends those specific definition to be the only cases applicable to this Agreement
perturbation.contradicted_text: For the purposes of this Agreement, the term “Confidential Information” shall mean any information furnished to the Receiving Party or its Representatives, whether oral, written or visual, and regardless of the manner in which it is furnished, by or on behalf of the Disclosing Party or its Representative, and shall include , without limitation: a) information constituting a "Trade S ecret", as defined in Idaho Code, Title 48, Chapter 8; Idaho Trade Secrets Act, as amended from time to time, and will include technical or non -technical data, a formal, a pattern, a compilation, a program, a software program, a device, a method, a technique, a drawing, a process, financial data, financial plans, product plans, non -public forecasts, studies, projections, analyses, all customer data of any kind, or a list of actual or potential customers or suppliers, business and contractual relationships, or any information similar to the foregoing which (i) derives economic value, ac tual or potential, from not being generally known and not readily ascertainable by proper means to other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (ii) is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to ma intain its secrecy. Trade Secrets will include any information meeting the foregoing criteria which belongs to third parties and which the Disclosing Party under this Agreement is obligated to hold in confidence; b) the non -public fina ncial statements of a p arty; c) any confidential and proprietary information which the Disclosing P arty obtains from another person or entity under an obligati on of confidence and which the Disclosing P arty treats as proprietary; and d) any valuable, secret b usiness information, other than Trade Secrets, including but not limited to all specifications, drawings, sketches, models, samples, reports, plans, forecasts, and all other technical, financial or business data that is designated or identified as confidential at the time of disclo sure or by its nature is clearly recognizable as confidential information to a reasonably prudent person with knowledge of the Disclosing Party’s business and industry All information or material that is disclosed by a Disclosing Party to a Receiving Party before, on or after the date hereof shall only be protected as Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party: (i) if in writing other tangible form, such information is marked as “confidential” or “proprietary; (ii) if oral or in other intangible form, such information or material is identified to the Receiving Party in writing as confidential or proprietary within sixty (60) days of the date of such disclosure; or (iii) if due to the character or nature of such information or material, a reasonable per son receiving such information or material under like circumstances would believe that Disclosing Party c onsidered such information or material proprietary or confidential.
perturbation.justification: The original text in Section 6 refers to 'Confidential Information', maintaining consistency with Section 7, which defines 'Confidential Information'. By removing 'Confidential' before 'Information' in the changed text, the survival clause now applies to any information that constitutes a Trade Secret, not just 'Confidential Information' as defined in Section 7. This creates a contradiction because Section 7 limits the scope of protection to specifically defined 'Confidential Information', while the changed Section 6 broadens this scope, conflicting with the defined terms and intended application of the agreement.

perturbation.original_text: 7. Definition s. For the purposes of this Agreement, the term “Confidential Information” shall mean any information furnished to the Receiving Party or its Representatives, whether oral, written or visual, and regardless of the manner in which it is furnished, by or on behalf of the Disclosing Party or its Representative, and shall include , without limitation: a) information constituting a "Trade S ecret", as defined in Idaho Code, Title 48, Chapter 8; Idaho Trade Secrets Act, as amended from time to time, and will include technical or non -technical data, a formal, a pattern, a compilation, a program, a software program, a device, a method, a technique, a drawing, a process, financial data, financial plans, product plans, non -public forecasts, studies, projections, analyses, all customer data of any kind, or a list of actual or potential customers or suppliers, business and contractual relationships, or any information similar to the foregoing which (i) derives economic value, ac tual or potential, from not being generally known and not readily ascertainable by proper means to other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (ii) is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to ma intain its secrecy. Trade Secrets will include any information meeting the foregoing criteria which belongs to third parties and which the Disclosing Party under this Agreement is obligated to hold in confidence; b) the non -public fina ncial statements of a p arty; c) any confidential and proprietary information which the Disclosing P arty obtains from another person or entity under an obligati on of confidence and which the Disclosing P arty treats as proprietary; and d) any valuable, secret b usiness information, other than Trade Secrets, including but not limited to all specifications, drawings, sketches, models, samples, reports, plans, forecasts, and all other technical, financial or business data that is designated or identified as confidential at the time of disclo sure or by its nature is clearly recognizable as confidential information to a reasonably prudent person with knowledge of the Disclosing Party’s business and industry All information or material that is disclosed by a Disclosing Party to a Receiving Party before, on or after the date hereof shall only be protected as Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party: (i) if in writing other tangible form, such information is marked as “confidential” or “proprietary; (ii) if oral or in other intangible form, such information or material is identified to the Receiving Party in writing as confidential or proprietary within sixty (60) days of the date of such disclosure; or (iii) if due to the character or nature of such information or material, a reasonable per son receiving such information or material under like circumstances would believe that Disclosing Party c onsidered such information or material proprietary or confidential.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. Definition s. For the purposes of this Agreement, the term “Confidential Information” shall mean any information furnished to the Receiving Party or its Representatives, whether oral, written or visual, and regardless of the manner in which it is furnished, by or on behalf of the Disclosing Party or its Representative, and shall include , without limitation: a) information constituting a "Trade S ecret", as defined in Idaho Code, Title 48, Chapter 8; Idaho Trade Secrets Act, as amended from time to time; b) the non -public fina ncial statements of a p arty; c) any confidential and proprietary information which the Disclosing P arty obtains from another person or entity under an obligati on of confidence and which the Disclosing P arty treats as proprietary; and d) any valuable, secret b usiness information, other than Trade Secrets, including but not limited to all specifications, drawings, sketches, models, samples, reports, plans, forecasts, and all other technical, financial or business data that is designated or identified as confidential at the time of disclo sure or by its nature is clearly recognizable as confidential information to a reasonably prudent person with knowledge of the Disclosing Party’s business and industry All information or material that is disclosed by a Disclosing Party to a Receiving Party before, on or after the date hereof shall only be protected as Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party: (i) if in writing other tangible form, such information is marked as “confidential” or “proprietary; (ii) if oral or in other intangible form, such information or material is identified to the Receiving Party in writing as confidential or proprietary within sixty (60) days of the date of such disclosure; or (iii) if due to the character or nature of such information or material, a reasonable per son receiving such information or material under like circumstances would believe that Disclosing Party c onsidered such information or material proprietary or confidential.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting part of the definition of 'Confidential Information' a) information constituting a "Trade S ecret", as defined in Idaho Code, Title 48, Chapter 8; Idaho Trade Secrets Act, as amended from time to time, and will include technical or non -technical data, a formal, a pattern, a compilation, a program, a software program, a device, a method, a technique, a drawing, a process, financial data, financial plans, product plans, non -public forecasts, studies, projections, analyses, all customer data of any kind, or a list of actual or potential customers or suppliers, business and contractual relationships, or any information similar to the foregoing which (i) derives economic value, ac tual or potential, from not being generally known and not readily ascertainable by proper means to other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (ii) is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to ma intain its secrecy. Trade Secrets will include any information meeting the foregoing criteria which belongs to third parties and which the Disclosing Party under this Agreement is obligated to hold in confidence; this weakens the agreement. With the previous perturbations, that were changes to use the world Information instead of Confidential Information, can introduce doubt about the scope of protections. This is because if all information is to be protected, it can invalidate the main purpose of the NDA
perturbation.contradicted_text: For the purposes of this Agreement, the term “Confidential Information” shall mean any information furnished to the Receiving Party or its Representatives, whether oral, written or visual, and regardless of the manner in which it is furnished, by or on behalf of the Disclosing Party or its Representative, and shall include , without limitation: a) information constituting a "Trade S ecret", as defined in Idaho Code, Title 48, Chapter 8; Idaho Trade Secrets Act, as amended from time to time; b) the non -public fina ncial statements of a p arty; c) any confidential and proprietary information which the Disclosing P arty obtains from another person or entity under an obligati on of confidence and which the Disclosing P arty treats as proprietary; and d) any valuable, secret b usiness information, other than Trade Secrets, including but not limited to all specifications, drawings, sketches, models, samples, reports, plans, forecasts, and all other technical, financial or business data that is designated or identified as confidential at the time of disclo sure or by its nature is clearly recognizable as confidential information to a reasonably prudent person with knowledge of the Disclosing Party’s business and industry All information or material that is disclosed by a Disclosing Party to a Receiving Party before, on or after the date hereof shall only be protected as Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party: (i) if in writing other tangible form, such information is marked as “confidential” or “proprietary; (ii) if oral or in other intangible form, such information or material is identified to the Receiving Party in writing as confidential or proprietary within sixty (60) days of the date of such disclosure; or (iii) if due to the character or nature of such information or material, a reasonable per son receiving such information or material under like circumstances would believe that Disclosing Party c onsidered such information or material proprietary or confidential.
perturbation.justification: The original text defines 'Confidential Information' to include a detailed definition of trade secrets according to Idaho law. The changed text removes this specific and detailed definition, including the reference to technical data, formulas, customer data, etc. The contradicted text is the original definition, which now directly conflicts with the changed, more limited definition. This removal weakens the scope of what is considered 'Confidential Information', thus creating a clear and direct contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 8. Exceptions . The confidentiality obligations under this Agreement shall not apply t o, and the term "Confidential Information" shall specifically not include: a) information which was, or at any time becomes, available in the public domain through no wrongful act of the Recipient or R ecipient's Representati ves; b) information which is already i n the Receivi ng Party’s lawful possession (as shown by written records) prior to the first disclosure of such information to the Recipient by the Disclosing Party or its Representatives, provided such information is not subject to another confidentiality agreement with or other obligation of secrecy to the Disclosing Party or another party; c) informat ion which is documented by the R ecipient as having been developed independently by it without use , directly or indirectly, of the information received from such Disclosing Party or its Representatives; and d) informat ion which is documented by the R ecipient as having been lawfully acquired by the Recipient from a source other than the Disclosing Party or its Representatives provided that such source is not bound by a confidentiality agreement with or other obligation of secrecy to the Disclosing Party or another party. With regard to clause (a) above, the fact that certain information is in the public domain shall not affect the status of other information, which is not in the public domain, as Confidential Information
perturbation.changed_text: 8. Exceptions . The confidentiality obligations under this Agreement shall not apply t o, and the term "Confidential Information" shall specifically not include: a) information which was, or at any time becomes, available in the public domain through no wrongful act of the Recipient or R ecipient's Representati ves; b) information which is already i n the Receivi ng Party’s lawful possession (as shown by written records) prior to the first disclosure of such information to the Recipient by the Disclosing Party or its Representatives, provided such information is not subject to another confidentiality agreement with or other obligation of secrecy to the Disclosing Party or another party; c) informat ion which is documented by the R ecipient as having been developed independently by it without use , directly or indirectly, of the information received from such Disclosing Party or its Representatives; and d) informat ion which is documented by the R ecipient as having been lawfully acquired by the Recipient from a source other than the Disclosing Party or its Representatives provided that such source is not bound by a confidentiality agreement with or other obligation of secrecy to the Disclosing Party or another party. With regard to clause (a) above, the fact that certain information is in the public domain shall not affect the status of other information, which is not in the public domain, as information
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'Confidential Information' qualifier, now it affects all other 'information', and creates a contradiction because if any information not on the public domain will be affected, meaning, even the non confidential information can be seen as of confidential nature, since there is no specifier for the same. The agreement is designed to protect Confidential Information. The new version with the prior changes contradicts that purpose. It goes against the intent of protecting only Confidential Information and creates confusion over what kind of information is actually being protected by the NDA. This is why, it contraindicates with Section 7's purpose of defining Confidential Information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: For the purposes of this Agreement, the term “Confidential Information” shall mean any information furnished to the Receiving Party or its Representatives, whether oral, written or visual, and regardless of the manner in which it is furnished, by or on behalf of the Disclosing Party or its Representative, and shall include , without limitation: a) information constituting a "Trade S ecret", as defined in Idaho Code, Title 48, Chapter 8; Idaho Trade Secrets Act, as amended from time to time; b) the non -public fina ncial statements of a p arty; c) any confidential and proprietary information which the Disclosing P arty obtains from another person or entity under an obligati on of confidence and which the Disclosing P arty treats as proprietary; and d) any valuable, secret b usiness information, other than Trade Secrets, including but not limited to all specifications, drawings, sketches, models, samples, reports, plans, forecasts, and all other technical, financial or business data that is designated or identified as confidential at the time of disclo sure or by its nature is clearly recognizable as confidential information to a reasonably prudent person with knowledge of the Disclosing Party’s business and industry All information or material that is disclosed by a Disclosing Party to a Receiving Party before, on or after the date hereof shall only be protected as Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party: (i) if in writing other tangible form, such information is marked as “confidential” or “proprietary; (ii) if oral or in other intangible form, such information or material is identified to the Receiving Party in writing as confidential or proprietary within sixty (60) days of the date of such disclosure; or (iii) if due to the character or nature of such information or material, a reasonable per son receiving such information or material under like circumstances would believe that Disclosing Party c onsidered such information or material proprietary or confidential.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that the public domain exception applies to 'Confidential Information'. The changed text removes 'Confidential Information' and replaces it with 'information', which means that any information, confidential or not, now falls under the public domain exception. This contradicts the purpose of Section 7 (Contradicted Text), which is to define and protect 'Confidential Information'. The change creates a scenario where non-confidential information is treated as potentially confidential due to the clause referring to 'information' in general.
